title: Organisational transformation precedes AI transformation - LinkedIn
search_path_label: A 级媒体 / GDELT 路径
full_text:
No AI transformation without organisational transformation?
No AI transformation without organisational transformation?
This TechCrunch piece on Anthropic and OpenAI launching enterprise AI service ventures caught my eye.
My take: the future isn’t about doing more digital transformation. It’s about shifting where effort goes, because where new value sits is moving.
Work shifts from systems to flow.
Historically, we’ve optimised within systems: make an app better, tighten a process, improve one platform. But value increasingly comes from orchestrating end-to-end work across systems, teams and data. People don’t care about “using system A”. They care about getting an outcome, safely, quickly and consistently.
Scale beats experimentation.
The world is full of pilots that never become real capability. The differentiator won’t be whether organisations experiment with AI agents. It will be whether they industrialise a few things that matter - and stop doing the rest.
Affordability becomes a design constraint.
Demand on digital keeps rising, but budgets rarely rise at the same pace. So the question becomes: do we keep adding cost and complexity, or redesign work so we get better outcomes for less?
Resilience moves upstream.
Resilience can’t just mean “recover faster”. It means prevent more, optimise continuously, and automate routine response safely. As systems become more complex, observability, control and accountability must be designed in.
The important shift is that new value is moving up the stack. Systems of record remain essential (we cannot run without them) but they are no longer where most new performance value will be created. Above them sit integration, data and controls: the layer that makes end-to-end journeys possible and automation safe. Above that sit orchestration, automation and agents: the layer that can coordinate work, apply rules and automate multi-step tasks. At the top are the operational outcomes we are trying to deliver.
Many organisations still focus most investment at the bottom: systems of record. That is understandable. But it is increasingly insufficient if value is moving higher up the stack. #SaaSpocolypse
The opportunity is not to do more, but to shift effort so we get better outcomes for less: prioritise end-to-end flows over system-by-system optimisation, scale a few things rather than run endless pilots, and treat integration, data quality, controls and governance as foundations for safe automation.
I’m old enough to remember EAI, BPM and microservices - each promising to fix organisational inefficiency. All were useful. None was a silver bullet. Agentic AI will make similar promises and will fall short of the hype. But it is also a step up from the integration and process tools we had at the turn of the century.
https://lnkd.in/eA4gm8JR
Anthropic and OpenAI are both launching joint ventures for enterprise AI services | TechCrunch

No AI transformation without organisational transformation?
No AI transformation without organisational transformation?
This TechCrunch piece on Anthropic and OpenAI launching enterprise AI service ventures caught my eye.
My take: the future isn’t about doing more digital transformation. It’s about shifting where effort goes, because where new value sits is moving.
Work shifts from systems to flow.
Historically, we’ve optimised within systems: make an app better, tighten a process, improve one platform. But value increasingly comes from orchestrating end-to-end work across systems, teams and data. People don’t care about “using system A”. They care about getting an outcome, safely, quickly and consistently.
Scale beats experimentation.
The world is full of pilots that never become real capability. The differentiator won’t be whether organisations experiment with AI agents. It will be whether they industrialise a few things that matter - and stop doing the rest.
Affordability becomes a design constraint.
Demand on digital keeps rising, but budgets rarely rise at the same pace. So the question becomes: do we keep adding cost and complexity, or redesign work so we get better outcomes for less?
Resilience moves upstream.
Resilience can’t just mean “recover faster”. It means prevent more, optimise continuously, and automate routine response safely. As systems become more complex, observability, control and accountability must be designed in.
The important shift is that new value is moving up the stack. Systems of record remain essential (we cannot run without them) but they are no longer where most new performance value will be created. Above them sit integration, data and controls: the layer that makes end-to-end journeys possible and automation safe. Above that sit orchestration, automation and agents: the layer that can coordinate work, apply rules and automate multi-step tasks. At the top are the operational outcomes we are trying to deliver.
Many organisations still focus most investment at the bottom: systems of record. That is understandable. But it is increasingly insufficient if value is moving higher up the stack. #SaaSpocolypse
The opportunity is not to do more, but to shift effort so we get better outcomes for less: prioritise end-to-end flows over system-by-system optimisation, scale a few things rather than run endless pilots, and treat integration, data quality, controls and governance as foundations for safe automation.
